file_name: ClickstreamCorp_20200330_1-A_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_12089935_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_Development Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: The Developer shall execute and deliver to the Client any instruments of transfer and take such other action that the Client may reasonably request, including, without limitation, executing and filing, at the Client's expense, copyright applications, assignments, and other documents required for the protection of the Client's rights to such materials.
perturbation.changed_text: The Developer shall execute and deliver to the Client any instruments of transfer at the Client's request.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit mention of copyright applications and related actions 'at the Client's expense,' the contract becomes vague regarding who bears the financial responsibility of securing intellectual property rights. This omission potentially contradicts California law, which mandates clear assignment of intellectual property rights and cost allocation, especially regarding copyright protection for commissioned works.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code - Section 2860: Indemnity as to future wrongful act; illegality
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code 2778(3) implicitly requires clarity in contractual obligations. By removing the phrase about the Client bearing the expense of copyright applications, the contract creates ambiguity and potential disputes, hindering the Developer's ability to fully protect the Client’s rights. This omission could lead to a legal claim that the agreement doesn't adequately ensure the client obtains full protection of its intellectual property rights, contrary to the intent of California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 2778. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2778. In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after request, the person indemnifying neglects to defend the person indemnified, a recovery against the latter suffered by him in good faith, is conclusive in his favor against the former; 6. If the person indemnifying, whether he is a principal or a surety in the agreement, has not reasonable notice of the action or proceeding against the person indemnified, or is not allowed to control its defense, judgment against the latter is only presumptive evidence against the former; 7. A stipulation that a judgment against the person indemnified shall be conclusive upon the person indemnifying, is inapplicable if he had a good defense upon the merits, which by want of ordinary care he failed to establish in the action. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly states that copyright applications and related actions are to be done 'at the Client's expense.' The modified contract removes this specification, creating ambiguity about who bears the financial responsibility of securing intellectual property rights. The Law Explanation suggests this omission contradicts California Civil Code 2778(3) which implicitly requires clarity in contractual obligations and could lead to a legal claim that the agreement doesn't adequately ensure the client obtains full protection of its intellectual property rights. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 2778, provides rules for interpreting indemnity contracts and highlights the importance of clearly defined obligations. By removing the explicit assignment of expense, the modified contract introduces ambiguity, weakening the client's protection, creating a legal loophole, and creating a legal contradiction as per the definition given in the prompt.

perturbation.original_text: The Client may assign this Agreement freely, in whole or in part. The Developer may not, without the written consent of the Client, assign, subcontract, or delegate its obligations under this Agreement, except that the Developer may transfer the right to receive any amounts that may be payable to it for its Services under this Agreement, which transfer will be effective only after receipt by the Client of written notice of such assignment or transfer.
perturbation.changed_text: The Client may assign this Agreement freely, in whole or in part. The Developer may not, without the written consent of the Client, assign, subcontract, or delegate its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception that allows the developer to transfer the right to receive payments, the contract now places an unreasonable restriction on the developer's ability to manage its receivables. California law disfavors contracts that unduly restrict the transfer of payment rights, particularly when it can create undue hardship for the transferring party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 697.740
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 697.740 provides protections related to judgment liens and the ability to transfer interest in property, and by extension, rights to payment. Removing the developer's right to transfer receivables creates a significant imbalance in the agreement and may be viewed as an unreasonable restriction on their ability to receive payment for services rendered. This could be argued as violating the spirit, if not the letter, of laws that protect the right to receive payment.

ENFORCEMENT OF MONEY JUDGMENTS [695.010 - 709.030] ( Division 2 added by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1364, Sec. 2. ) CHAPTER 2. Liens [697.010 - 697.920] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1364, Sec. 2. ) ARTICLE 4. Execution Lien [697.710 - 697.750] ( Article 4 added by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1364, Sec. 2. ) 697.740. Except as provided in Sections 9617 and 9622 of the Commercial Code and in Section 701.630, if personal property subject to an execution lien is not in the custody of a levying officer and the property is transferred or encumbered, the property remains subject to the lien after the transfer or encumbrance except where the transfer or encumbrance is made to one of the following persons: (a) A person who acquires an interest in the property under the law of this state for reasonably equivalent value without knowledge of the lien. For purposes of this subdivision, value is given for a transfer or encumbrance if, in exchange for the transfer or encumbrance, property is transferred or an antecedent debt is secured or satisfied. (b) A buyer in ordin
perturbation.justification: The original contract allowed the developer to transfer the right to receive payments, subject to providing written notice to the Client. The changed contract removes this right, creating a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this removal places an unreasonable restriction on the developer's ability to manage receivables, potentially violating laws that protect the right to receive payment. The Law Explanation references California Code of Civil Procedure Section 697.740, indicating legal protections related to transferring interest in property and rights to payment. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 697.740, discusses property transfer and encumbrances, suggesting that restrictions on the transfer of receivables could be problematic under California law. The omission of the developer's right to transfer receivables, therefore, creates a significant imbalance and a potential legal issue.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the state of Florida. If litigation results from or arises out of this Agreement or the performance thereof, each Party shall be responsible for its own attorneys' fees, court costs, and all other expenses, whether or not taxable by the court as costs.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the state of Florida. If litigation results from or arises out of this Agreement or the performance thereof, each Party shall be responsible for its own court costs.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the language regarding attorneys' fees, the contract implicitly suggests that neither party can recover attorneys’ fees, even if a statute or common law principle would otherwise allow it. This is particularly problematic because the contract relates to application development and intellectual property, where fee-shifting provisions are common in infringement cases, especially under the Copyright Act. The contract's omission could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent federal law regarding attorney fee awards in copyright litigation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 17 U.S. Code § 505 - Remedies for infringement: Costs and attorney's fees
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S.C. § 505 allows courts to award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party in copyright infringement cases. Since the contract involves the creation of a mobile application (which is copyrightable), the omission of language regarding attorney's fees creates a potential conflict with this federal statute. If the Client were to infringe the Developer’s copyright (or vice-versa), the contract’s wording might be incorrectly interpreted to bar the prevailing party from recovering attorney’s fees, hindering the statute's purpose.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 U.S. Code § 505 - Remedies for infringement: Costs and attorney’s fees | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 505 - Remedies for infringement: Costs and attorney’s fees U.S. Code Notes prev | next In any civil action under this title, the court in its discretion may allow the recovery of full costs by or against any party other than the United States or an officer thereof. Except as otherwise provided by this title, the court may also award a reasonable attorney’s fee to the prevailing party as part of the costs. ( Pub. L. 94–553, title I, § 101 , Oct. 19, 1976 , 90 Stat. 2586 .) Historical and Revision Notes house report no. 94–1476 Under section 505 the awarding of costs and attorney’s fees are left to the court’s discretion, and the section also makes clear that neither costs nor attorney’s fees can be awarded to or against “the United States or an officer thereof.” U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the provision stating that each party is responsible for their own attorneys' fees, while still holding each party responsible for its own court costs. The law explanation and perturbation explanation both highlight that in copyright infringement cases, 17 U.S.C. § 505 allows courts to award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states, "the court may also award a reasonable attorney’s fee to the prevailing party as part of the costs." By omitting language about attorney's fees, the modified contract could be interpreted to prevent a prevailing party from recovering these fees in a copyright dispute related to the application, directly contradicting the provision allowing for such recovery as stated in the law snippet.
